items.title: Use `bisect`, don't hand-roll
items.avoid: Writing your own binary search for production code.
items.why: The stdlib version is in C, handles edge cases correctly, and is read by reviewers as 'binary search' at a glance.

items.title: Pick one interval convention
items.avoid: Mixing half-open `[lo, hi)` with closed `[lo, hi]` in the same function.
items.prefer: Half-open by default; `hi = len(xs)` and `hi = mid`.
items.why: Mixed conventions are the #1 source of off-by-one binary-search bugs.
items.example:
```python
lo, hi = 0, len(xs)
while lo < hi:
    mid = lo + (hi - lo) // 2
    if xs[mid] < t: lo = mid + 1
    else:           hi = mid
```

items.title: Test the predicate is monotonic before binary-searching
items.avoid: Assuming any yes/no function is binary-searchable.
items.prefer: Sample the predicate at a few points and confirm it's monotonic.
items.why: Binary search on a non-monotonic predicate is silently wrong.
items.example:
```python
assert all(p(a) <= p(b) for a, b in zip(samples, samples[1:]))
```

items.title: Use sortedcontainers for streams with many inserts
items.why: insort itself is O(n) (shifting); SortedList keeps inserts logarithmic.
items.example:
```python
from sortedcontainers import SortedList
sl = SortedList()
for x in stream(): sl.add(x)
```

items.title: Reach for a hash table when order doesn't matter
items.avoid: Sorting + bisecting purely for membership tests.
items.prefer: A set for membership, a dict for keyed values.
items.why: O(1) average beats O(log n) and avoids the sort cost.
items.example:
```python
stop = set(stopwords)
if word in stop: ...
```
